content.text: When an agentic AI system is retired, two things are at risk of permanent loss: the explicit knowledge captured in code, documentation, and datasets, and the tacit knowledge held by the engineers and operators who built and ran the system. A disciplined archival and knowledge transfer process preserves both, giving successor teams, regulators, and future researchers access to what was learned — even years after the system has shut down.
What It Is

Archival and knowledge transfer on sunsetting is the process of capturing, packaging, and handing off everything a retiring agentic AI system knows and does. It covers source code, architecture documents, model weights, training datasets, operational runbooks, post-mortems, and the informal expertise that lives in the team's heads. The goal is not just to back up files — it is to ensure that a team member who never worked on the system can understand and potentially reconstruct it from the archive.
Why It Matters
Successor systems are built faster when teams can study what the previous system did, what it got wrong, and what prompts, tools, and architectures worked.
Regulatory and legal investigations can arise years after a system is retired — an auditable archive is the evidence you need.
Tacit knowledge ("why we chose this chunking strategy" or "the prompt that eliminated 80% of hallucinations") disappears when the team disbands if not captured explicitly.
Model weights and training datasets from a retired system may be valuable inputs for fine-tuning future models.
Enterprise customers who integrated with the retired system may need architectural documentation to understand their own past decisions.
Benefits
A complete archive makes incident investigations into past behaviour possible without relying on memory.
Well-documented architectural decisions reduce re-discovery cost for teams building similar systems later.
Captured prompts and evaluation datasets are directly reusable when prompting the successor model.
Knowledge transfer sessions surface undocumented operational patterns that would otherwise be lost.
A searchable archive reduces the time-to-answer for support questions about the retired system from hours to minutes.
Trade-offs
Knowledge transfer requires time from the team at exactly the moment when they are being reassigned to new projects — there is always scheduling pressure.
Large model weights (tens of gigabytes) and training datasets (terabytes) are expensive to store long-term even in cold storage.
Documentation written under time pressure at end-of-life is often lower quality than documentation written incrementally during the project.
Archives that are not indexed and searchable become write-only stores — they exist but nobody can find anything in them.
How It Works

The archive and transfer process moves through five steps: inventory and categorize knowledge assets, capture tacit knowledge from team members, format and package everything into durable formats, store with access controls, and validate that the archive is actually retrievable.
Inventory:
List every artifact — repositories, wikis, model registry entries, runbooks, evaluation datasets, prompt libraries, post-mortems, ADRs (Architecture Decision Records), and design documents.
Capture tacit knowledge:
Conduct structured exit interviews with key team members. Record video walkthroughs of the system. Hold a retrospective specifically focused on undocumented decisions and operational tricks.
Format and package:

Convert artifacts to durable formats — Markdown for documents, Parquet for tabular datasets, JSON for structured configs, and a Git bundle for the repository. Avoid formats that require proprietary tools to read.
Store with access controls:
Move the packaged archive to cold object storage with a metadata index. Assign access roles: compliance team, data science team, platform team, future researchers.
Validate retrievability:
Restore a sample of each artifact type and confirm it is readable. Search the metadata index for a specific decision and confirm it surfaces. Run the code from the bundle in a clean environment.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Archival and knowledge transfer architecture: knowledge sources flow into a five-step archive process, then to transfer recipients and a long-term archive store containing code, doc, data, and model archives

content.text: The diagram shows six knowledge source categories flowing into the five-step archive process, producing four archive stores: code (Git bundle), documents (searchable knowledge base), data (Parquet on cold storage), and models (weights plus configs).
When to Use It
Any agentic system being shut down or replaced, regardless of size.
Team restructuring where the engineers who built the system move to different projects.
Mergers or acquisitions where one company's AI systems are consolidated into the other's.
Model version deprecations where the fine-tuned weights and training data have future value.
Research projects transitioning from exploration to production, where the exploration-era code and experiments should be preserved.
How to Implement
Step 1: Create a Git Bundle for Code Archival

#!/bin/bash
# Archive a Git repository to a portable bundle that contains all branches and tags.
# The bundle can be cloned, pulled, or fetched like a remote — no server required.

REPO_PATH="/path/to/agent-system"
BUNDLE_NAME="agent-system-archive-$(date +%Y%m%d).bundle"
S3_BUCKET="s3://code-archive-7yr"

cd "$REPO_PATH"

# Create a bundle with ALL refs (branches, tags, notes)
git bundle create "$BUNDLE_NAME" --all

# Verify the bundle is valid
git bundle verify "$BUNDLE_NAME"
echo "Bundle created: $BUNDLE_NAME"

# Upload to cold storage
aws s3 cp "$BUNDLE_NAME" "$S3_BUCKET/code/" \
  --storage-class GLACIER_IR \
  --sse aws:kms

echo "Uploaded to $S3_BUCKET/code/$BUNDLE_NAME"

# Later, to restore the bundle:
# git clone agent-system-archive-20260101.bundle restored-agent-system
Step 2: Export Architecture Decision Records

"""Export all ADRs and design documents from Confluence to a local Markdown archive."""
import requests
import re
from pathlib import Path

CONFLUENCE_BASE = "https://your-company.atlassian.net/wiki"
SPACE_KEY = "AGENT"  # Confluence space containing agent documentation
API_TOKEN = "your-api-token"  # from environment

def export_confluence_space(output_dir: Path) -> list[str]:
    output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    session = requests.Session()
    session.auth = ("user@example.com", API_TOKEN)

    start = 0
    exported = []

    while True:
        resp = session.get(
            f"{CONFLUENCE_BASE}/rest/api/content",
            params={"spaceKey": SPACE_KEY, "start": start, "limit": 50, "expand": "body.storage"},
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()

        for page in data["results"]:
            title = re.sub(r"[^\w\-]", "_", page["title"])
            body_html = page["body"]["storage"]["value"]
            # In practice, use html2text or pandoc for HTML-to-Markdown conversion
            out_path = output_dir / f"{title}.md"
            out_path.write_text(f"# {page['title']}\n\n{body_html}", encoding="utf-8")
            exported.append(str(out_path))

        if data["start"] + data["limit"] >= data["totalSize"]:
            break
        start += data["limit"]

    return exported

pages = export_confluence_space(Path("knowledge-archive/docs"))
print(f"Exported {len(pages)} pages")
Step 3: Archive Model Weights with Provenance Metadata

import json
import boto3
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

def archive_model_weights(
    model_dir: Path,
    archive_bucket: str,
    model_id: str,
    provenance: dict,
) -> str:
    """
    Upload model weights to cold storage with a provenance metadata sidecar.
    Provenance records what data was used for training, by whom, and under what licence.
    """
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    archive_prefix = f"models/{model_id}/{datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d')}/"

    # Upload each file in the model directory
    for file_path in sorted(model_dir.rglob("*")):
        if file_path.is_file():
            key = archive_prefix + file_path.relative_to(model_dir).as_posix()
            s3.upload_file(
                str(file_path),
                archive_bucket,
                key,
                ExtraArgs={"StorageClass": "GLACIER_IR", "ServerSideEncryption": "aws:kms"},
            )

    # Upload provenance sidecar alongside weights
    provenance_key = archive_prefix + "provenance.json"
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=archive_bucket,
        Key=provenance_key,
        Body=json.dumps(provenance, indent=2),
        ContentType="application/json",
    )

    return f"s3://{archive_bucket}/{archive_prefix}"

location = archive_model_weights(
    model_dir=Path("./checkpoints/agent-v3-final"),
    archive_bucket="model-archive-7yr",
    model_id="agent-v3",
    provenance={
        "base_model": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        "training_data": "s3://training-datasets/agent-v3/",
        "training_date": "2025-06-01",
        "trainer": "ml-team@example.com",
        "licence": "internal-only",
        "evaluation_results": "s3://eval-results/agent-v3/final-eval.json",
    },
)
print(f"Model archived to {location}")
Examples
Example 1: Exit Interview Question Template

## Agent System Exit Interview

Interviewer: ____  Engineer: ____  Date: ____

### System Overview
- What did this system do that the documentation does NOT capture?
- What would you change if you rebuilt it today?

### Operational Knowledge
- What recurring incidents are not in the runbooks?
- Which alerts were noise and could be disabled?
- What is the most common cause of quality regressions?

### Prompts and Models
- Which system prompt changes had the biggest positive impact?
- What evaluation benchmarks were most predictive of real-world quality?
- Were there prompts we tried that consistently failed and should not be retried?

### Data
- Are there training or evaluation datasets not listed in the model card?
- Which data sources were unreliable and excluded from training?

### Dependencies
- Which vendor APIs were most fragile?
- What integrations required undocumented workarounds?

### Lessons Learned
- What would you warn the next team to avoid?
- What should the successor system definitely keep?
Example 2: Archive Metadata Index

import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

def build_archive_index(archive_root: str, output_path: str) -> None:
    """
    Build a searchable JSON index of all archived artifacts.
    Each entry records the artifact's location, type, date, and summary.
    """
    index = []

    # In practice, you would query S3 for object metadata
    # and parse README/manifest files for summaries.
    entries = [
        {
            "id": "code-bundle-20260101",
            "type": "code",
            "location": "s3://code-archive-7yr/code/agent-system-archive-20260101.bundle",
            "archived_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
            "summary": "Full repository bundle including all branches, tags, and history.",
            "tags": ["python", "fastapi", "anthropic-sdk", "agentic"],
            "size_gb": 2.3,
        },
        {
            "id": "docs-confluence-20260101",
            "type": "documentation",
            "location": "s3://code-archive-7yr/docs/",
            "archived_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
            "summary": "142 Confluence pages covering architecture, runbooks, and ADRs.",
            "tags": ["architecture", "adr", "runbook", "design"],
            "size_gb": 0.04,
        },
        {
            "id": "model-agent-v3-20260101",
            "type": "model",
            "location": "s3://model-archive-7yr/models/agent-v3/20260101/",
            "archived_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
            "summary": "Final production model weights with training provenance sidecar.",
            "tags": ["fine-tuned", "claude-3-5-sonnet", "agent"],
            "size_gb": 14.7,
        },
    ]

    Path(output_path).write_text(json.dumps(entries, indent=2))
    print(f"Index written with {len(entries)} entries to {output_path}")

build_archive_index(
    archive_root="s3://code-archive-7yr/",
    output_path="knowledge-archive/index.json",
)
Real-World Example

When Facebook Research retired FBLearner Flow — their first-generation machine learning platform — in favour of the internal PyTorch-based platform, they ran a multi-month knowledge transfer process that included recorded walkthroughs of every component, a porting guide for teams migrating existing models, and a freeze of the FBLearner source in a read-only archive accessible to all internal engineers. The tacit knowledge capture, done through structured interviews with the original engineers, surfaced a number of configuration quirks and workarounds that were not in any document — and these were documented explicitly to prevent the successor platform from making the same mistakes.
Common Mistakes
Treating archival as a last-minute task — by the time the system shuts down, key engineers have already left or moved on and the knowledge is gone.
Archiving code without archiving the environment — a Git bundle without the pinned dependency list and Docker base image is not reproducible.
Skipping the retrievability validation — write-only archives that have never been tested for restore are not archives, they are landfills.
Not archiving evaluation benchmarks alongside model weights — weights without the eval suite that validated them cannot be compared to future models.
Storing the archive index in the same bucket as the archive — if the bucket is deleted or access is lost, the index is gone with it.
Neglecting to document the licence and usage rights of training data — future teams cannot safely reuse the data without knowing whether they are allowed to.
Best Practices
Start the knowledge capture process at least 2 months before shutdown — before team members are formally reassigned.
Write ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) throughout the system's life, not just at sunset — they are far more accurate when written close to the decision.
Use durable, open formats: Markdown for text, Parquet for tables, JSON for structured data, Git bundles for code. Avoid proprietary formats that require specific software versions to read.
Include a
README.md
at the root of every archive that explains what the system did, why it was retired, and how to navigate the archive.
Record exit interviews as video as well as text — tone, emphasis, and body language carry information that transcripts lose.
Create a designated archive owner who is responsible for the archive's accessibility for the legally mandated period, even if they never worked on the original system.
Test the archive annually: pick a random artifact and confirm it is still retrievable and readable.
Performance and Scalability

Large model weights and training datasets require parallel upload pipelines. Use S3 Transfer Manager or gsutil's parallel upload mode for multi-part transfers. For very large datasets (terabytes), consider AWS Snowball Edge for physical transfer to avoid network bottlenecks. Index the archive with a metadata database (DynamoDB or a simple SQLite file) rather than relying on S3 key listing, which slows down with millions of objects.
Use S3 multipart upload for files larger than 100 MB — individual part failures can be retried without re-uploading the entire file.
Compress large text datasets with Zstandard (
zstd
) before archiving — it gives better ratios than gzip and is faster to decompress.
Partition Parquet datasets by date or category so specific subsets can be restored without reading the entire archive.
Tooling and Ecosystem
git bundle
— built-in Git command for creating a portable, single-file archive of a repository.
DVC (Data Version Control)
— tracks and versions large ML datasets and model files alongside Git, enabling consistent archival.
MLflow Model Registry
— records model lineage, parameters, and metrics in a structured format that survives model retirement.
Hugging Face Hub
— public or private model repository with built-in versioning and model card support.
Obsidian / Notion / MkDocs
— knowledge management tools for building a searchable internal documentation archive.
adr-tools
— CLI for creating and managing Architecture Decision Records in Markdown.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Tacit Knowledge
Explicit Knowledge
Cost
Structured archive + transfer (this guide)
Captured via interviews
Fully archived
Medium effort upfront
Code-only backup
None
Code only — no docs or weights
Low
Handoff document only
Partial
High-level only
Very low
Shadow period (successor team runs both)
High — live mentoring
High
Very high (dual staffing)
Security and Reliability
Encrypt all archived artifacts at rest with a KMS key whose access policy is scoped to the archive owner role — not the full engineering org.
Use S3 Object Lock (Compliance mode) for compliance-class archived artifacts to prevent any party from deleting them before the retention period expires.
Store the archive index in a system separate from the archive itself — if the S3 bucket is accidentally deleted, you still need to know what was in it.
Perform a retrievability test on a sample of archived artifacts every 12 months — cold storage media can degrade and format readers can become unavailable.
Apply the principle of least privilege to archive access: compliance archives should be readable only by legal and compliance teams, not all engineers.
Key Takeaways
Archival and knowledge transfer must start at least 2 months before shutdown — waiting until the final week means the team has already dispersed and the tacit knowledge is gone.
Tacit knowledge (undocumented decisions, operational tricks, failed experiments) disappears when the team disbands unless it is captured through structured exit interviews and recorded walkthroughs.
Use durable, open formats: Markdown, Parquet, JSON, and Git bundles. Proprietary formats risk becoming unreadable as tooling evolves.
Archive model weights alongside their provenance metadata — weights without training data lineage and evaluation results cannot be safely reused or compared.
A write-only archive is worthless. Validate retrievability before calling the archival complete: restore a sample, search the index, run the code.
Designate an archive owner — a named person responsible for access and integrity for the full retention period, even if they never worked on the original system.
Include a top-level README in every archive package that explains what the system did, why it was retired, what to look for, and who to contact.
